Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I'd block out time to study, maybe 16 hours a week or so, but I rarely stuck to a perfect schedule. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making notes and rewriting key concepts in my own words. Some weeks I was really on top of things, other weeks my anxiety would spike and I'd struggle to focus even when I had the time set aside.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and how I was feeling mentally. When assignments were due, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might slack off a bit. My attendance wasn't great—around half the classes, honestly—so I relied heavily on catching up through readings and the LMS materials when I could push through the anxiety.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was checking if there were new assignments posted or if grades had come back. I'd also log in when I felt that familiar wave of anxiety about falling behind—it was almost like a compulsion to check that I hadn't missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch videos when I couldn't bring myself to attend class in person.
Q4: Yeah, there were stretches where logging in became almost automatic, especially mid-semester when things picked up. I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning with my coffee, just scanning for updates. It became part of my routine because missing something would send my anxiety through the roof, so staying connected felt like a way to keep control.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me—I watched over four hours of them total. Since my attendance was spotty, those videos were my lifeline to actually understanding the material. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could engage with peers without the pressure of face-to-face interaction, which my anxiety made difficult.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional readings and some of the supplementary resources. When my anxiety was high, I had to triage—I'd focus only on what was directly tied to assignments or exams. If something felt like extra work without clear payoff, I just couldn't justify the mental energy it would take to engage with it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or improving my understanding of core concepts. If I could tell it would help with an assignment or the exam, I was in. But if it felt vague or like busy work, my brain would just reject it—especially when I was already fighting to stay on top of things.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them, so I'd push through even when anxious. Optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later rarely came. There was always something more urgent demanding my attention.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download readings and PDFs, then study them offline for hours. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd print things out, annotate them, rewrite notes by hand. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, with textbooks and my own handwritten summaries spread across my desk.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep in study mode—going through printed notes, working through problems on paper, or reading a textbook chapter—and none of that showed up in the LMS. My best thinking often happened offline. I'd spend hours synthesizing information in ways that left zero digital footprint.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. I preferred having materials saved locally so I didn't have to rely on internet connection or deal with the LMS interface repeatedly. One download could fuel several study sessions.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when my anxiety peaked. Surprisingly, even though my stress was generally low, the anxiety could be paralyzing. I'd look at the workload and feel overwhelmed, even if objectively it was manageable. During those times, even logging in felt like too much.
Q13: Fatigue and anxiety were the big ones for me. When I was exhausted or my mind was racing, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes pulled my attention away too. I'd have to make hard choices about where to put my limited energy.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be physically present on the platform but mentally checked out—worrying about other things or just too anxious to focus. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed things more than I'd like to admit, especially tasks that felt overwhelming or ambiguous. Even knowing something was important, my anxiety would make me avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt calmer, but that calm rarely came. It's a frustrating cycle I'm still working on breaking.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike right before due dates—I'd suddenly be very active, submitting work, checking requirements, watching videos I'd put off. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. Deadlines gave me structure that my anxious brain desperately needed.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going and engage more. But if feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it could trigger my anxiety and make me want to withdraw for a bit. I had to work through those feelings before re-engaging.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to anchor to. But when the structure was loose or expectations were unclear, I'd struggle. I needed that scaffolding because my own internal organization isn't always reliable when anxiety is running high.
Q19: Clear instructions and well-organized modules made engagement easier. What made it harder was when resources were scattered or when there were too many optional paths—my brain would get overwhelmed trying to figure out what actually mattered. Simplicity and clarity were my friends in this course.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about the material. They might keep checking the LMS, rewatching videos, reading and rereading—but struggle to actually produce work because of fear of failure or perfectionism. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that never showed up in the logs. Someone could appear inactive but be working hard with printed materials or textbooks. The LMS only captures one slice of how students actually learn and engage with content.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you're actually understanding or just scrolling. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of thinking happening behind the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement feels like being mentally present and invested—which is harder than it sounds when anxiety is constantly pulling my attention elsewhere.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still managing to complete most of my assignments. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes or the mental effort it took just to log in some days. My resilience isn't in the data—just the outcomes that I fought hard to achieve.
